'''Cameron Wayne Smith''' {{post-nominals|country=AUS|AM}} (born 18 June 1983) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|hk}}, spending his entire career with the [[Melbourne Storm]] in the [[National Rugby League]] (NRL). He has also been an assistant coach of the [[Queensland rugby league team]] under former Melbourne teammate [[Billy Slater]] since 2022.

An {{rlnt|Australia}}n international and [[Queensland rugby league team|Queensland]] [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] representative, Smith is regarded as one of the greatest players of all time.<ref>{{cite web|last=Balym|first=Todd|date=3 December 2013|title=Kangaroos captain Cameron Smith belongs in the list of rugby league all time greats|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/kangaroos-captain-cameron-smith-belongs-in-the-list-of-rugby-league-all-time-greats/news-story/4787090b7b5fb6324620bb7393b6f26d|work=Fox Sports|access-date=9 August 2018|archive-date=9 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200209060117/https://www.foxsports.com.au/kangaroos-captain-cameron-smith-belongs-in-the-list-of-rugby-league-all-time-greats/news-story/4787090b7b5fb6324620bb7393b6f26d|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Honeysett|first=Stuart|date=7 September 2017|title=Rugby league Immortal Andrew Johns anoints Cameron Smith as the greatest player he's ever seen|url=https://wwos.nine.com.au/2017/09/07/13/47/andrew-johns-anoints-cameron-smith-as-greatest-ever-nrl-player|publisher=Wide World of Sports|access-date=9 August 2018|archive-date=9 August 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180809121955/https://wwos.nine.com.au/2017/09/07/13/47/andrew-johns-anoints-cameron-smith-as-greatest-ever-nrl-player|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Moore|first=Andrew|date=29 September 2017|title=Cameron Smith's greatest season at 34 shows why he is an all-time great and a future Immortal|newspaper=ABC News|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2017-09-28/dallym-win-underlines-cam-smith-as-one-of-all-time-greats-moore/8996350|publisher=ABC|access-date=9 August 2018|archive-date=30 September 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170930003555/http://www.abc.net.au/news/2017-09-28/dallym-win-underlines-cam-smith-as-one-of-all-time-greats-moore/8996350|url-status=live}}</ref> During his nineteen-year career, Smith won the [[Dally M Medal]] as the NRL's player of the year in 2006 and 2017, the [[Rugby League World Golden Boot Award|Golden Boot Award]] as the international player of the year in 2007 and 2017, and the NRL's Dally M Hooker of the Year on nine occasions.{{efn|Smith won the Dally M Hooker of the Year in 2006, 2008, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2016, 2017, 2019 and 2020.}} He was the [[captain (sports)|captain]] of the Storm for fifteen years,{{efn|Smith was one of the multiple captains used by Melbourne in 2006 and 2007 as part of their rotating captaincy system; he was their designated captain in the Grand Final of both of those seasons. Smith was the club's sole captain from 2008 to 2020.}} during which time they won the 2012, 2017, and 2020 NRL Premierships,{{efn|Not included are Melbourne's 2007 and 2009 Grand Finals wins, which were stripped due to [[Melbourne Storm salary cap breach|long-term salary cap breaches]].}} and also Australia and Queensland from 2012{{efn|Officially named as captain of both Queensland and Australia from 2012 onwards, Smith had previously acted as captain in his predecessor [[Darren Lockyer]]'s absence in all three games of the [[2008 State of Origin series|2008 series]] for Queensland, and on five occasions for Australia between 2007 and 2011.}} until his retirement from representative matches in 2018.

Considered a future [[The Immortals (rugby league)|Immortal]],<ref>{{cite web|last=Logue|first=Matt|date=10 March 2021|title=How long does Cameron Smith have to wait before he becomes an Immortal?|url=https://www.news.com.au/sport/nrl/how-long-does-cameron-smith-have-to-wait-before-he-becomes-an-immortal/news-story/b8a24b57795fb901b7dce9b8504ed8f5|publisher=news.com.au|access-date=11 April 2012|archive-date=10 March 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210310082539/https://www.news.com.au/sport/nrl/how-long-does-cameron-smith-have-to-wait-before-he-becomes-an-immortal/news-story/b8a24b57795fb901b7dce9b8504ed8f5|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Masters|first=Roy|date=11 March 2021|title=Smith announced himself as an Immortal early on – now it's just a matter of time|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/smith-announced-himself-as-an-immortal-early-on-now-it-s-just-a-matter-of-time-20210311-p579s6.html|publisher=Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=11 April 2021|archive-date=10 April 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210410153950/https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/smith-announced-himself-as-an-immortal-early-on-now-it-s-just-a-matter-of-time-20210311-p579s6.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Smith is currently the NRL's games record holder, with 430 matches played;<ref name=":0" /> he is the only player to have played over 400 matches. He is also the highest all-time points scorer in NRL history, having surpassed [[Hazem El Masri]]'s 2,418 points on 12 April 2019.<ref>{{cite web|last=Wolfgram|first=Jason|date=12 April 2019|title=Smith breaks point-scoring record as Storm beat Cowboys|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2019/04/12/cameron-smith-breaks-point-scoring-record-as-storm-beat-cowboys/|publisher=NRL.com|access-date=23 October 2019|archive-date=12 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190412200814/https://www.nrl.com/news/2019/04/12/cameron-smith-breaks-point-scoring-record-as-storm-beat-cowboys/|url-status=live}}</ref> Smith also holds the NRL records for most goals kicked (1295), most tackles made (16917), most grand final points scored (44) and most wins (310).<ref>{{cite web |last1=Walter |first1=Brad |title=If this was Captain Cam's swan song he hit the perfect note |date=25 October 2020 |url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2020/10/25/if-this-was-captain-cams-swan-song-he-hit-the-perfect-note/ |publisher=National Rugby League |access-date=25 October 2020 |archive-date=28 October 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201028210318/https://www.nrl.com/news/2020/10/25/if-this-was-captain-cams-swan-song-he-hit-the-perfect-note/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Gardiner |first1=Gilbert |title=Cameron Smith, the humble everyday champion who has given so much to Victoria |url=https://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/cameron-smith-the-humble-everyday-champion-who-has-given-so-much-to-victoria/news-story/b0378ec5fd5037de19cfd96dba617548 |website=Herald Sun |publisher=News Corp |access-date=5 November 2020}}</ref> Smith debuted in the NRL as a {{rlp|hb}} and he also played as a {{rlp|fe}} during his career.

Smith was appointed a [[Member of the Order of Australia]] (AM) in the [[2019 Queen's Birthday Honours (Australia)|2019 Queen's Birthday Honours]] for his service to rugby league.<ref name=honours>{{Cite web|url=https://honours.pmc.gov.au/honours/awards/2004840|title=Cameron Wayne Smith|website=honours.pmc.gov.au|access-date=2019-06-10|archive-date=2 August 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210802121346/https://honours.pmc.gov.au/honours/awards/2004840|url-status=live}}</ref> He retired from professional rugby league in 2021.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|last=Ward|first=Roy|date=2021-03-10|title='The right time to finish': Storm great Smith retires from rugby league|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/melbourne-storm-great-cameron-smith-retires-from-rugby-league-20210310-p579e3.html|access-date=2021-03-10|website=The Sydney Morning Herald|language=en|archive-date=13 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813063449/https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/melbourne-storm-great-cameron-smith-retires-from-rugby-league-20210310-p579e3.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

==Early life==
Smith was born on 18 June 1983 in [[Logan City|Logan]], Queensland, Australia. He attended [[Marsden State High School]].<ref>[http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/school-days-to-class-acts/story-e6frfgbo-1225702277172 School days to class acts] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110614153641/http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/school-days-to-class-acts/story-e6frfgbo-1225702277172 |date=14 June 2011 }} By Matt Marshall (''[[Herald Sun]]'' 23 April 2009)</ref>

==Playing career==
[[File:Cameron Smith.JPG|thumb|right|Smith on international duty with Australia in 2006]]

=== Early Career (2000–2001) ===
Smith played at {{rlp|hk|fe}} for the [[Logan Brothers Rugby League Football Club|Logan Brothers]] in [[Logan City, Queensland|Logan City]]. After scoring 188 points for the [[Norths Devils]] in the [[Queensland Cup]]<ref name="NORTHS DEVILS">{{cite web|url=http://www.qrl.com.au/default.aspx?s=qldcup-clubs-norths|title=NORTHS DEVILS|publisher=QRL|year=2010|access-date=15 March 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110218231418/http://www.qrl.com.au/default.aspx?s=qldcup-clubs-norths|archive-date=18 February 2011}}</ref> he moved up to the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] with the [[Melbourne Storm]] club.

=== Joining the Storm (2002–2003) ===
Smith's first two games for Melbourne were during the [[2002 NRL season]], both played at half-back (No.7). Returning to the Queensland Cup, he won the Norths Devils player of the year award in 2002.<ref>{{cite web |title=Club Honour Board - Norths Devils |url=https://northsdevilsrlfc.com/about/club-honour-board/ |website=northsdevilsrlfc.com/ |access-date=22 February 2022 |archive-date=22 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220222235508/https://northsdevilsrlfc.com/about/club-honour-board/ |url-status=live }}</ref> The following year he played as the Storm's hooker. In 2003, the [[Queensland rugby league team]] were without a specialist hooker. After experimenting with different players in the position for the first two matches of the [[2003 State of Origin series]] and losing both, Smith was named at hooker (No.9) for the third match. For the next six years, no other player wore the number 9 for the Maroons until this run of 19 consecutive Origins was cut due to injury. He was named the Melbourne Storm's rookie of the year in 2003.<ref>{{cite web|last=melbournestorm.com.au|title=NRL Honour Board|url=http://www.melbournestorm.com.au/default.aspx?s=nrl-honour-board|work=Club|publisher=[[Melbourne Storm]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130928161940/http://www.melbournestorm.com.au/default.aspx?s=nrl-honour-board|archive-date=28 September 2013}}</ref>

=== Early success and Representative selection (2005–2007) ===
[[File:Cameron Smith 2007.jpg|thumb|Smith with the Storm in 2007]]
In 2005, Smith was named the Melbourne Storm's player of the year. The following year he first represented Australia and won the [[Dally M Medal]]. Also in Round 3 of 2006 Smith captained the club for the first time and in what became a very successful year for him as later that year he led the Storm to the [[2006 NRL Grand Final]]. During the Grand Final, while the game was still in the balance, he was forced from the field with injury, which the Storm's loss has been largely attributed to.<ref>{{cite news
| last = Masters
| first = Roy
| title = Broncos shine on centre stage
| work = The Sydney Morning Herald
| publisher = Fairfax digital
| date = 2 October 2006
| url = http://www.smh.com.au/news/league/broncos-shine-on-centre-stage/2006/10/01/1159641214513.html
| access-date = 16 December 2009
| archive-date = 5 November 2012
| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20121105132142/http://www.smh.com.au/news/league/broncos-shine-on-centre-stage/2006/10/01/1159641214513.html
| url-status = live
}}</ref>
At the Melbourne Storm awards night, Smith was named the Storm's 2006 player of the year.{{cn|date=May 2025}}

Smith was selected to play for the [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian national team]] at hooker the 2007 [[ANZAC Test]] match victory against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Anzac Test Match 2007|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/anzac-test-match-2007/australia-vs-new-zealand.html|work=rugbyleagueproject.org|publisher=Shawn Dollin, Andrew Ferguson and Bill Bates|access-date=1 September 2013|archive-date=24 October 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181024072758/http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/anzac-test-match-2007/australia-vs-new-zealand.html|url-status=live}}</ref> For the [[2007 State of Origin series]], Smith took out both the Wally Lewis Medal for player of the series and Ron Mcauliffe Medal for Queensland player of the series.<ref>{{cite news
|last=Livermore
|first=Ross
|title=Australian Rugby Football League Annual Report 2007
|pages=9
|publisher=Australian Rugby League Limited
|year=2007
|url=http://www.australianrugbyleague.com.au/files/ARL_2007_Annrep_1.pdf
|access-date=15 July 2009
|url-status=dead
|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090913145959/http://www.australianrugbyleague.com.au/files/ARL_2007_Annrep_1.pdf
|archive-date=13 September 2009
}}</ref> He was also named the Melbourne Storm player of the year. Smith captained the Melbourne Storm in their 34–8 victory over the [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]] in the [[2007 NRL Grand Final]].

=== Becoming captain (2008–2009) ===
Smith became regular captain of the Melbourne Storm in 2008. Smith captained the Maroons for the entire [[2008 State of Origin series]] due to the absence of regular captain [[Darren Lockyer]]. The Maroons won the series, despite losing the first match 18–10. In August 2008, Smith was named in the [[Australia national rugby league team#Rugby League World Cup Squad|preliminary 46-man Kangaroos squad]] for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]],<ref>{{cite news|author=Liam FitzGibbon|publisher=Fox Sports News (Australia)|title=Surprises in Kangaroos squad|url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24111780-23214,00.html|access-date=2 August 2008|date=1 August 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080808144730/http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0%2C8659%2C24111780-23214%2C00.html|archive-date=8 August 2008}}</ref><ref name="Veteran Lockyer named in Australian squad">{{cite news|url=http://www.iht.com/articles/ap/2008/08/01/sports/AS-RGL-Australia-Squad.php|title=Veteran Lockyer named in Australian squad|newspaper=International Herald Tribune|date=1 August 2008|access-date=2 August 2008|archive-date=16 September 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080916064704/http://www.iht.com/articles/ap/2008/08/01/sports/AS-RGL-Australia-Squad.php|url-status=live}}</ref> and in October 2008 he was selected in the final 24-man [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#Australia|Australia]] squad.<ref name="Manly six win Australia call-up">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7656211.stm|title=Manly six win Australia call-up|publisher=BBC|date=7 October 2008|access-date=7 October 2008|archive-date=10 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081010140001/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7656211.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> Cameron was suspended for two weeks in the 2008 finals series after a grapple tackle on Brisbane's [[Sam Thaiday]]. The suspension would have only been one week had he not have carried over points from a previous grapple tackle in round 1 of the year's competition. This meant he was suspended for Melbourne's [[2008 NRL Grand Final|2008 grand final]]. Melbourne Storm CEO Brian Waldron and coach [[Craig Bellamy (rugby league)|Craig Bellamy]] claimed the judiciary had made their minds up before Cameron's case was heard.{{cn|date=February 2025}} The judiciary panel members then threatened to sue the club if the comments were not retracted. Cameron signed a four-year extension to his contract in December 2008, resulting in him being a contracted player through to the end of at least the 2012 season. His contract is rumoured to be partly funded by third party sponsors who do not contribute to the standard salary cap.{{cn|date=February 2025}} He became the Melbourne Storm's most experienced playing player due to the retirement of [[Matt Geyer]] at the end of 2008. He became the last current Storm player in the squad who played under [[Mark Murray (rugby league)|Mark Murray]] as well as the only one to have played in a season when the Storm did not make the finals due to their performance (the Storm finished 10th in 2002, the year Smith made his debut).
[[File:Ashley Harrison, Ben Hannant, Cameron Smith, Petero Civoniceva and Sam Thaiday (25 June 2009, Cairns).jpg|thumb|right|Smith (centre) training with [[Queensland rugby league team|the Maroons]] in 2009]]
In April 2009, he was named in the preliminary 25 man squad to represent Queensland in the opening [[Rugby League State of Origin|State of Origin]] match for 2009.<ref>{{cite news|work=[[Fox Sports (Australia)|Fox Sports]]|url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,25398645-5016947,00.html|title=Queensland name preliminary State of Origin squad|access-date=28 April 2009|date=28 April 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120918133045/http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,25398645-5016947,00.html|archive-date=18 September 2012 }}</ref> He was selected for Australia in the one-off test match against New Zealand on 8 May 2009.<ref>{{cite news|work=[[Fox Sports (Australia)|Fox Sports]]|title=Kangaroos selectors stick by losing World Cup team for New Zealand Test|first=Tom|last=Wald|url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,25422175-5016947,00.html|date=3 May 2009|access-date=3 May 2009|archive-date=4 December 2012|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121204183728/http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,25422175-5016947,00.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In the preliminary final in which Melbourne defeated the [[Brisbane Broncos]] 40–10, he became Storm's highest ever point scorer, beating [[Matt Orford]]'s record of 877. Smith led the Storm in their [[2009 NRL Grand Final]] victory over the [[Parramatta Eels]].<ref>[http://www.nrl.com/telstrapremiership/telstrapremiershipscores/tabid/10240/roundid/833/fixtureid/5801/infotabid/3/default.aspx Telstra Premiership Scores] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091003020642/http://www.nrl.com/telstrapremiership/telstrapremiershipscores/tabid/10240/roundid/833/fixtureid/5801/infotabid/3/default.aspx|date=3 October 2009 }} </ref> At the end of the season, he joined the Kangaroos on their campaign to win the [[2009 Four Nations]] tournament in England. He scored the try that saved Australia from losing in the opening match and also scored in the Kangaroos' victory against England in the final.

Many of Smith's club achievements between the years of 2006 and 2010 were later placed under an asterisk after the Melbourne Storm were stripped of all honours achieved in those years due to the discovery of [[Melbourne Storm salary cap breach|salary cap breaches]] within the club.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.theage.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/melbourne-storm-stripped-of-everything-20100422-tfy6.html|title=Melbourne Storm stripped of everything|last=Munro|first=Ian|date=23 April 2010|newspaper=The Age|access-date=23 April 2010|archive-date=24 April 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100424075449/http://www.theage.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/melbourne-storm-stripped-of-everything-20100422-tfy6.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== National captaincy and Premiership success (2010–2012) ===
In 2010, he played in the [[2010 World Club Challenge]] at halfback in the absence of his usual number 7 [[Cooper Cronk]] due to injury. Smith kicked 5 goals and was named man of the match. For the [[2010 ANZAC Test]] victory, Smith was selected to play at hooker. For the first time since Game II of the 2003 Origin series, Smith wasn't wearing the number 9 for Queensland, after he failed to overcome the elbow injury he sustained playing in that test. He was replaced by [[Matt Ballin]].<ref>{{cite news| url=http://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/sport/ballin-proves-bennett-very-wrong/story-e6frg7mf-1225870278696| first=Dan| last=Koch| title=Matt Ballin proves Wayne Bennett happily wrong| date=24 May 2010| work=The Australian}}</ref> In Round 26 of the [[2010 NRL season]] Smith became the first Melbourne Storm player to reach 1,000 points after successfully converting [[Ryan Hinchcliffe]]'s try. 
[[File:Camsmith2010.jpg|thumb|right|Smith playing for the Storm in 2010]]
Smith played in the 42–0 thrashing of the [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team]] during the [[2010 Four Nations]]. It was a game where he converted 5 tries and scored once. He was later named man of the match.

Smith was named man-of-the-match in Queensland's win in Game I and later Game III of the [[2011 State of Origin series]], subsequently resulting in his naming as the Wally Lewis medal winning man-of-the-series. On 3 November 2011 The annual [[RLIF Awards]] dinner was held at the [[Tower of London]] and Smith was named hooker of the year.<ref>{{cite news|title=Slater scoops player award|url=http://www1.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/15313/7289077/Slater-scoops-player-award|access-date=5 November 2011|newspaper=[[Sky Sports]]|date=3 November 2011|archive-date=4 November 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111104043623/http://www1.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/15313/7289077/Slater-scoops-player-award|url-status=live}}</ref>

Following [[Darren Lockyer]]'s retirement at the end of the 2011 season, Smith became captain of both Australia and Queensland. He captained both sides to the [[2012 ANZAC Test]] and [[2012 State of Origin series]] victories respectively. At the [[2012 Dally M Awards]] Smith was named the NRL's hooker of the year.<ref>{{cite news|last=News Limited|author-link=News Limited|title=All the Dally M winners plus galleries|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/fans-believe-ben-barba-will-score-a-runaway-win-in-the-dally-m-award/story-e6frfgbo-1226465030839|access-date=5 September 2012|newspaper=[[Herald Sun]]|date=4 September 2012}}</ref> He captained Storm to victory in the [[2012 NRL Grand Final]] over the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]]. Smith also received the [[Rugby League International Federation]]'s international player of the year award for 2012.<ref>{{cite web|title=Awards|url=http://www.rlif.com/awards|work=rlif.com|publisher=[[Rugby League International Federation]]|access-date=5 December 2013|archive-date=3 October 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111003072304/http://www.rlif.com/awards|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== World Cup success (2013–2016) ===
[[File:Cam Smith.jpg|thumb|right|upright=1.3|Smith playing for the Kangaroos at [[Anfield]] in 2016]]
In early 2013, Smith captained the Storm to victory in the [[2013 World Club Challenge]] over [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] earning the title of world champions.
Smith captained Australia in the [[2013 ANZAC Test]] and played at hooker. He kicked four goals from six attempts in what was the first [[Test match (rugby league)|test match]] ever played in [[Canberra]].
After victory in the third and deciding game of the [[2013 State of Origin series]] Smith was awarded the [[Wally Lewis Medal]] for player of the series, in which he captained the [[Queensland rugby league team|Queensland team]] which extended their record winning streak to 8 years.

On 9 June 2013, Smith played his 250th game for the Melbourne Storm, the second only player to have achieved that feat.<ref name="CooperCronk">{{cite news|title=Cooper Cronk notches up 250 games with Melbourne Storm|url=http://tvnz.co.nz/rugby-league-news/cronk-reflects-250-game-milestone-6065936|date=31 August 2014|access-date=31 August 2014|work=TV NZ|archive-date=23 September 2014|archive-url=https://archive.today/20140923163301/http://tvnz.co.nz/rugby-league-news/cronk-reflects-250-game-milestone-6065936|url-status=live}}</ref>

Queensland's win in Game III of the [[2014 State of Origin series]] made Smith the first player in State of Origin history to win 20 games.{{cn|date=May 2025}}

On 30 November 2013, Smith captained Australia to a dominant 34 to 2 win against New Zealand in the [[2013 Rugby League World Cup final]] ensuring the Australian side went through the entire tournament undefeated.

In November 2014, in the [[2014 Rugby League Four Nations|Four Nations]] game against England, Smith became just the sixth player to appear for Australia in 40 test matches.{{cn|date=May 2025}}

On 9 July 2015, Smith captained Queensland to a 2 to 1 series win against New South Wales winning game three emphatically by 52 – 6. It was Smiths' 9th series win in ten years and his fourth series win as captain. At series end, Smith had equalled Darren Lockyer for most Queensland appearances with 36 and received his fifth man of the match award in game one to sit in equal second behind Wally Lewis for most man of the match awards in State of Origin.

On 17 July 2015, Smith became the Melbourne Storms' first 300-game player and only the 24th player in the history of the NRL to achieve such a feat. As of April 2017, Smith is one of only three current NRL players to have eclipsed 300 NRL games.

On 22 June 2016, Smith captained Queensland to their 10th series win in 11 years over New South Wales. Smith was awarded his sixth man of the match award in game 2 of Origin and was awarded the Wally Lewis medal as the player of the series for the fourth time at series end.

=== Setting records, World Cup and Premiership success (2017–2019) ===
On 10 March 2017, Smith reached 2000 career points in a match against the [[New Zealand Warriors]], becoming the first forward, the first Queenslander, and just the fifth player in history to have achieved the milestone.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Brunsdon|first1=Simon|title=Warriors v Melbourne Storm: Milestone men Cameron Smith and Shaun Johnson rewrite the record books|url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/live-nrl-warriors-v-melbourne-storm-scores-updates-blog/news-story/1a099eff1248078255364cb30a7f2b8e|website=Fox Sports Australia|date=10 March 2017|publisher=News Corporation|access-date=10 March 2017|archive-date=12 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312214411/http://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/live-nrl-warriors-v-melbourne-storm-scores-updates-blog/news-story/1a099eff1248078255364cb30a7f2b8e|url-status=live}}</ref>

On 30 July 2017, Smith became only the 3rd player in NRL history to play 350 games, behind [[Darren Lockyer]] (355) and equal with [[Terry Lamb]]. On 9 September, he played his 356th match and thereby became NRL's most capped player of all time.

On 2 December 2017, he captained the Kangaroos to another World Cup victory, defeating [[England national rugby league team|England]] in the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup final|final]] 6–0. He was also named in the team of the tournament.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Layne|first=Max|date=2017-12-04|title=Rugby League World Cup Team of the Tournament|url=http://www.thegurgler.com/2017/12/04/rlwc-2017-team-of-the-tournament/|access-date=2020-10-26|website=The Gurgler|language=en-US|archive-date=2 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200802151946/http://www.thegurgler.com/2017/12/04/rlwc-2017-team-of-the-tournament/|url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:SmithWCC2018.jpg|thumb|right|Smith holding the [[2018 World Club Challenge|2018 WCC]] trophy aloft]]
On 30 March 2018, during the round 4 fixture against the Sharks, Smith was sent to the [[Penalty box|sin bin]] for backchatting. This was Smith's first sin-binning in his career and the Storm lost the game 14–4 in a match that had 33 penalties in total.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/teams/sharks/storm-skipper-cameron-smith-sinbinned-for-the-first-time-in-his-career/news-story/932dc949136a1d9145eda67671783f9a |title=Storm skipper Cameron Smith sinbinned for the first time in his career |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

On 15 May 2018, Smith announced his retirement from all representative football, making him unavailable for Queensland or Australian selection.<ref>[http://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-05-15/cameron-smith-makes-shock-queensland-and-australia-retirement/9762780 Cameron Smith makes shock Queensland and Australian representative retirement] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210802122047/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-05-15/cameron-smith-makes-shock-queensland-and-australia-retirement/9762780 |date=2 August 2021 }} ''[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]]'' 15 May 2018</ref>

In July 2019, he became the first NRL player to play 400 games. The ''[[Herald Sun]]'' marked the occasion with a front-page photograph by Mark Stewart at AAMI Park showing Smith and the only four others to achieve the feat in Australia, all [[Australian rules football]]ers: [[North Melbourne Football Club|North Melbourne]]'s [[Brent Harvey]] (the AFL/VFL leader in games played with 432), [[Essendon Football Club|Essendon]]'s [[Dustin Fletcher]], [[Hawthorn Football Club|Hawthorn]]'s [[Michael Tuck]] and [[Richmond Football Club|Richmond]]'s [[Kevin Bartlett (Australian rules footballer)|Kevin Bartlett]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Robinson |first1=Mark |title=''AFL legends welcome Smith into elite club''|url=https://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/afl/expert-opinion/mark-robinson/afl-legends-welcome-melbourne-storm-superstar-cam-smith-into-the-400-club/news-story/22ea2883d3356d9585be9f9a5bf8ccfc |publisher=[[Herald Sun]] |access-date=14 June 2022}}</ref> For perspective, just two NRL or AFL players has since replicated the feat: [[Shaun Burgoyne]] of [[Port Adelaide Football Club|Port Adelaide]] and Hawthorn in 2021, and [[Scott Pendlebury]] of {{AFL Col}} in 2024.

=== More Premiership success in COVID-affected year and retirement (2020–2021) ===
The Melbourne Storm [[2020 NRL season]] saw Smith captain the team for his 13th consecutive year. The season was suspended indefinitely on 23 March 2020 due to the ongoing [[COVID-19 pandemic|coronavirus pandemic]]; however, on 28 April 2020, the NRL announced it would recommence on 28 May as a 20-round competition with a revised draw. During the season, Smith landed 86 goals and scored a total of 184 points.

In Round 9, the Storm defeated the Canberra Raiders 20–14, with Smith reaching another milestone with 300 wins in the NRL. In Round 12, the Storm defeated the Knights in their 6th consecutive win, with Smith injuring his shoulder while scoring a try.

On 25 October 2020, Smith captained the Storm to another NRL Premiership, scoring 14 points including a try in a 26–20 Grand Final victory over the Penrith Panthers.<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2020/oct/25/nrl-grand-final-2020-melbourne-storm-penrith-panthers-match-report Melbourne Storm hold off late Penrith charge in grand final to claim fourth NRL premiership] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230204120324/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2020/oct/25/nrl-grand-final-2020-melbourne-storm-penrith-panthers-match-report |date=4 February 2023 }} ''[[The Guardian]]'' 25 October 2020</ref>

On 10 March 2021, the day before the start of the NRL season, Smith announced his retirement from professional rugby league.<ref name=":0" />

== Post playing ==
=== Hall of Fame ===
In August 2024, the [[National Rugby League]] announced that Smith was an inductee into the [[National Rugby League Hall of Fame]]. Smith, who was ascribed Hall of Fame number 120, was amongst eleven male players in the 2024 Class.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 Aug 2024 |title=NRL Hall of Fame 2024 Class |url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2024/08/14/nrl-hall-of-fame-2024-class/ |access-date=14 Aug 2024 |website=NRL |publisher=NRL Media Release}}</ref>

In Early 2025, Smith was named a co-host for ''100% Footy.''<ref>{{Cite web |title=Cameron Smith joins 100% Footy in 2025 |url=https://www.nineforbrands.com.au/media-release/cameron-smith-joins-100-footy-in-2025/ |access-date=2025-11-07 |website=Nine for Brands |language=en-AU}}</ref>

On 6 November 2025 it was announced that Smith had joined the coaching staff of the Brisbane Broncos.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-11-06 |title=Broncos’ stunning Smith coup; Manly hero’s last-ditch DCE bid: Off-season Central |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/nrl-2025-offseason-central-news-cameron-smith-joins-the-broncos-suncorp-stadium-to-host-world-cup-final-johnathan-thurston-buddy-frankling-new-york-marathon-news/news-story/fecc2f98825ccc95a0ecf0e0606fbdb6 |access-date=2025-11-07 |website=Fox Sports |language=en}}</ref>

== Personal life ==
Smith has three children with his long-term partner Barbara Johnson, whom he married in late 2011: two daughters and a son,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/melbourne-storm-captain-cameron-smith-a-knockabout-bloke/story-e6frfgbo-1226064691200|title=Melbourne Storm captain Cameron Smith a knockabout bloke}}</ref> he also supports the [[Hawthorn Football Club]],<ref>[http://www.foxsports.com.au/afl/afl-premiership/melbourne-storm-captain-cameron-smith-believes-hawthorn-can-climb-the-summit-and-win-the-premiership/story-e6frf3e3-1226600481435 Melbourne Storm captain Cameron Smith believes Hawthorn can climb the summit and win the premiership] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230312101006/https://www.foxsports.com.au/afl/melbourne-storm-captain-cameron-smith-believes-hawthorn-can-climb-the-summit-and-win-the-premiership/story-e6frf3e3-1226600481435?nk=eb22ac63768297aa3bca57bf13763c23-1678615806 |date=12 March 2023 }}, ''Fox Sports'', 19 March 2013</ref> and is an avid car enthusiast who is an ambassador for [[Holden Special Vehicles]] as well as an ambassador for the Ovarian Cancer Awareness campaign.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://m.facebook.com/cameron9smith/posts/900601816626916?comment_id=1134954799858282&comment_tracking=%257B%2522tn%2522%253A%2522R%2522%257D|title=Comments|website=m.facebook.com|access-date=26 June 2016|archive-date=12 March 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230312101004/https://m.facebook.com/login.php?next=https%3A%2F%2Fm.facebook.com%2Fcameron9smith%2Fposts%2F900601816626916%3Fcomment_id%3D1134954799858282%26comment_tracking%3D%25257B%252522tn%252522%25253A%252522R%252522%25257D&refsrc=deprecated&_rdr|url-status=live}}</ref>
